title: Ash-e reshteh
subtitle: Persian herb and noodle soup with chickpeas and lentils
excerpt: A dense, heavily herbed Persian soup loaded with chickpeas, kidney beans, lentils, and egg noodles, coloured green by an enormous quantity of spinach and herbs, and finished with caramelised onions, dried fenugreek, and a swirl of thick yoghurt. Eaten at Persian New Year as a symbol of good fortune.
sourceNotes: Ash-e reshteh is one of the oldest Persian soups, documented in Margaret Shaida's 'The Legendary Cuisine of Persia' (1992) and Najmieh Batmanglij's 'Food of Life' (1986). It is traditionally made for Persian New Year (Nowruz) and served at gatherings. The noodles symbolise the threads of fate. The word 'reshteh' means both 'noodle' and 'thread of fate'. The original uses Persian flat noodles; egg noodles are the best substitute.

content.text: Ash-e reshteh is a thick soup, almost closer to a stew. It should be dense with legumes, deeply green from the herbs and spinach, and substantial enough to be a meal on its own. The finishing toppings are not optional garnishes: the caramelised onion adds sweetness and texture, the dried fenugreek (kashouri methi) adds a distinct aromatic note, and the yoghurt provides the creamy contrast that pulls the whole bowl together.

The noodles go in at the end. They absorb liquid quickly and will turn the soup to paste if added too early. Break egg noodles into shorter lengths before adding. The soup thickens considerably as it sits, so add water when reheating.

items.name: Onion
items.prepNote: 2 finely chopped for the soup, 1 for the topping

content.text: Heat 3 tablespoons of the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} in a large pot. Cook 2 chopped {{onion}} for 12 minutes until soft and golden. Add {{turmeric}} and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{lentils-green}} and 1.5 litres of water. Bring to the boil, skim, and simmer for 20 minutes. Add the {{chickpeas-tinned}}, {{kidney-beans}}, all the fresh herbs: {{spinach}}, {{parsley-flat}}, {{coriander}}, {{chives}}, and salt. Simmer for 20 minutes.
Add noodles and caramelise the onions

Add the {{noodles-egg}} to the soup and cook for 10 minutes until tender. Meanwhile, heat the remaining {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} in a frying pan and cook the remaining sliced {{onion}} over medium-high heat for 15 to 20 minutes until deeply golden and crispy. Stir the {{fenugreek-leaves}} into the finished soup. Serve in bowls topped with the crispy onion and a generous spoonful of {{plain-yoghurt}}.

items.fix: Add boiling water a ladleful at a time, stirring between additions. The soup should be thick but pourable, not solid
items.symptom: Soup is too thick and stodgy

items.fix: The herb quantity is not optional. It IS the soup. Add more chopped parsley and coriander. The base onion must be cooked to golden before adding liquid
items.cause: Not enough herbs or onions not well cooked
items.symptom: Soup tastes vegetable but lacks depth

items.fix: Add noodles for the last 10 minutes only. They continue cooking in the residual heat so pull off the heat when they are just done
items.cause: Added too early or cooked too long

items.fix: Add yoghurt as a dollop on top of individual servings rather than stirring it through the hot pot
items.cause: Yoghurt too cold or soup too hot
items.symptom: Yoghurt curdles when added to the hot soup

content.text: Ash-e reshteh is the soup of Persian New Year, cooked in enormous quantities at the turn of spring and eaten for luck and sustenance. The noodles represent the threads of fate, cutting the noodles is considered bad luck, so they are left as long as practical. The soup is one of the defining dishes of Persian hospitality: rich, complex, and made with extraordinary generosity of ingredients. It is also the soup made for those who are sick, for those who have suffered a loss, and for the feeding of crowds. In its combination of legumes, greens, noodles, and tangy yoghurt, it is a complete meal from a single bowl.
